_comment: Disease → treatment & prevention recommendations. Keys match class folder names.

healthy.title: Plant Healthy
healthy.summary: No disease detected. Your crop looks healthy!
actions: Maintain current watering and fertilisation schedule.
Continue regular scouting every 7–10 days.
Remove any weeds that compete for nutrients.
Keep field records for future comparison.

cassava_mosaic.title: Cassava Mosaic Disease (CMD)
cassava_mosaic.summary: Caused by Cassava Mosaic Virus (CMV), spread by whitefly. Can cause 20–80% yield loss.
actions: Immediately rogue out and destroy severely infected plants.
Use certified CMD-resistant varieties (e.g., TME 204, NASE 14).
Control whitefly vectors with neem-based sprays or approved insecticides.
Plant clean, disease-free cuttings from certified sources.
Avoid moving planting material from affected to healthy areas.

cassava_bacterial_blight.title: Cassava Bacterial Blight (CBB)
cassava_bacterial_blight.summary: Caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. manihotis. Affects leaves, stems and vascular tissue.
actions: Remove and burn infected plants and plant debris.
Use clean, pathogen-free cuttings.
Avoid working in fields when plants are wet.
Apply copper-based bactericides as a preventive measure.
Use resistant varieties where available.

cassava_brown_streak.title: Cassava Brown Streak Disease (CBSD)
cassava_brown_streak.summary: Caused by Cassava Brown Streak Virus; whitefly-transmitted. Causes distinctive brown streaks on stems and root necrosis.
actions: Use CBSD-tolerant/resistant varieties (e.g., NASE 19, Kiroba).
Obtain planting material from certified, virus-tested sources.
Control whitefly population with neem oil or approved systemic insecticides.
Rogue infected plants early before the virus spreads further.
Avoid replanting from field that had CBSD.

maize_necrosis.title: Maize Lethal Necrosis (MLN)
maize_necrosis.summary: Caused by co-infection of MCMV and SCMV. One of the most destructive maize diseases in East Africa.
actions: Remove and destroy all infected plants immediately.
Control thrips and aphids (MLN vectors) with systemic insecticides.
Plant MLN-tolerant hybrids (WEMA tolerant hybrids, H614D).
Avoid ratooning maize in affected areas.
Observe a maize-free period of at least 3 months in severely affected areas.

maize_common_rust.title: Maize Common Rust
maize_common_rust.summary: Caused by Puccinia sorghi. Circular to elongated brown-orange pustules on leaves.
actions: Plant rust-resistant hybrids suited to your region.
Apply foliar fungicides (e.g., mancozeb, propiconazole) at early infection.
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation which promotes rust development.
Scout regularly and act early when <5% of leaf area is affected.
Remove heavily infected leaves to reduce spore load.

maize_gray_leaf_spot.title: Maize Gray Leaf Spot (GLS)
maize_gray_leaf_spot.summary: Caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. Grey, rectangular lesions running parallel to leaf veins.
actions: Use GLS-resistant hybrids.
Rotate crops — avoid consecutive maize seasons on same plot.
Incorporate or remove crop residues after harvest.
Apply fungicide (strobilurin-based) when disease appears before silking.
Ensure adequate plant spacing for air circulation.

maize_northern_leaf_blight.title: Northern Leaf Blight (NLB)
maize_northern_leaf_blight.summary: Caused by Exserohilum turcicum. Long, grey-green cigar-shaped lesions on upper leaves.
actions: Rotate with non-grass crops (beans, soybeans).
Plant resistant hybrids with Ht resistance genes.
Apply mancozeb or propiconazole at first sign of lesions.
Manage crop residues by deep ploughing or burning after harvest.
Avoid overhead irrigation that promotes leaf wetness.

maize_streak_virus.title: Maize Streak Virus (MSV)
maize_streak_virus.summary: Transmitted by leafhoppers. Causes characteristic yellow streaks along leaf veins.
actions: Plant MSV-resistant varieties (most commercial hybrids have resistance).
Control leafhopper vectors with insecticides at emergence.
Plant during cooler seasons when leafhopper populations are low.
Remove infected plants early to reduce spread.
Avoid planting near infected grass weeds and old maize fields.

banana_wilt.title: Banana Xanthomonas Wilt (BXW)
banana_wilt.summary: Caused by Xanthomonas vasicola pv. musacearum. The most devastating banana disease in East/Central Africa.
actions: Single Diseased Stem Removal (SDSR): cut out wilted stems at ground level.
Sterilise machetes and tools with 20% bleach solution between plants.
Use male bud removal to eliminate the main infection pathway.
Remove and bury all infected plant material.
Plant clean, BXW-free suckers or in vitro plantlets from certified sources.

banana_fusarium_wilt.title: Banana Fusarium Wilt (Panama Disease / TR4)
banana_fusarium_wilt.summary: Caused by Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense. Causes internal yellowing and wilting; no chemical cure.
actions: Quarantine infected fields — do not move soil or plant material.
Plant TR4-resistant varieties (e.g., FHIA-01, NARITA).
Improve soil drainage; the pathogen thrives in waterlogged soils.
Monitor for early wilting symptoms and act immediately.
Report suspected TR4 outbreaks to local agricultural authorities.

banana_sigatoka.title: Black Sigatoka
banana_sigatoka.summary: Caused by Pseudocercospora fijiensis. Yellow-brown leaf streaks progressing to leaf death.
actions: Apply approved fungicides (triazoles, strobilurins) on a calendar schedule.
Remove and destroy infected leaves (leaf stripping).
Ensure good drainage and avoid overhead irrigation.
Plant tolerant varieties where available.
Maintain adequate plant spacing to reduce humidity.

tomato_late_blight.title: Tomato Late Blight
tomato_late_blight.summary: Caused by Phytophthora infestans. Water-soaked lesions that rapidly turn brown; can destroy a crop in days.
actions: Apply systemic fungicides (metalaxyl+mancozeb) preventively in wet weather.
Remove and dispose of infected plant parts immediately.
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the base of plants.
Use resistant varieties (e.g., Heinz 1350, Shanty).
Rotate crops and do not plant tomato/potato together.

tomato_early_blight.title: Tomato Early Blight
tomato_early_blight.summary: Caused by Alternaria solani. Dark brown spots with concentric rings ('target' pattern) on lower leaves.
actions: Remove lower infected leaves and dispose of them away from the field.
Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicide as a preventive spray.
Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash spreading spores.
Use resistant hybrids where possible.
Avoid working in fields when plants are wet.

tomato_bacterial_spot.title: Tomato Bacterial Spot
tomato_bacterial_spot.summary: Caused by Xanthomonas spp. Small, water-soaked spots on leaves, fruit, and stems.
actions: Use copper-based bactericides (copper hydroxide) at first symptom.
Use certified disease-free seed and transplants.
Avoid overhead irrigation; drip irrigation is preferable.
Remove and destroy infected plant material.
Apply mancozeb+copper fungicide combination in severe cases.

tomato_leaf_mold.title: Tomato Leaf Mold
tomato_leaf_mold.summary: Caused by Passalora fulva. Pale green-yellow spots on upper leaves; olive-green mould on undersides.
actions: Improve greenhouse/field ventilation to reduce humidity.
Apply chlorothalonil or copper-based fungicide when symptoms appear.
Remove severely infected leaves.
Avoid wetting foliage; use drip irrigation.
Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Heinz 1350 in open field).

tomato_septoria_leaf_spot.title: Tomato Septoria Leaf Spot
tomato_septoria_leaf_spot.summary: Caused by Septoria lycopersici. Small, circular spots with dark borders and lighter centres.
actions: Remove infected lower leaves as soon as symptoms appear.
Mulch to reduce soil splash.
Apply mancozeb or chlorothalonil fungicide on a 7–10 day schedule.
Rotate crops — avoid solanaceous crops for at least 2 years.
Use stakes/trellises to improve air circulation.

tomato_spider_mites.title: Tomato Two-Spotted Spider Mite
tomato_spider_mites.summary: Two-spotted spider mite (Tetranychus urticae). Yellow stippling on leaves; fine webbing on underside.
actions: Apply acaricide (abamectin, spiromesifen) at first sign of stippling.
Spray under leaves — that is where mites live.
Maintain adequate soil moisture — drought stress worsens mite outbreaks.
Introduce predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) as biological control.
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural enemies.

tomato_target_spot.title: Tomato Target Spot
tomato_target_spot.summary: Caused by Corynespora cassiicola. Brown, concentric ring spots on leaves, stems, and fruit.
actions: Apply fungicide (azoxystrobin, chlorothalonil) when conditions are warm and humid.
Remove infected plant material.
Improve canopy aeration through proper pruning and staking.
Rotate crops with non-solanaceous plants.
Use resistant varieties when available.

tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.title: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV)
tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.summary: Transmitted by whitefly Bemisia tabaci. Severe yellowing, leaf curl, and stunted plants.
actions: Control whitefly with imidacloprid or thiamethoxam systemic insecticides.
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and reduce whitefly populations.
Plant TYLCV-resistant varieties (Tylka F1, Bellfort F1).
Use reflective silver mulch to deter whitefly.
Remove and destroy infected plants early to reduce virus reservoir.

tomato_mosaic_virus.title: Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV)
tomato_mosaic_virus.summary: Mechanically transmitted; causes mosaic mottling and distorted fruit.
actions: Use certified ToMV-resistant seed (Tm-2 gene varieties).
Wash hands and sterilise tools frequently.
Do not work with tobacco before handling tomato plants.
Remove and destroy infected plants.
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation.

potato_late_blight.title: Potato Late Blight
potato_late_blight.summary: Caused by Phytophthora infestans. Water-soaked lesions on leaves; rapidly destroys haulm and tubers.
actions: Apply metalaxyl-mancozeb or cymoxanil fungicides preventively in cool, wet conditions.
Destroy infected haulm before harvest.
Use certified seed tubers from disease-free sources.
Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Shangi, Markies, Unica).
Hill up to protect tubers from rain-splashed spores.

potato_early_blight.title: Potato Early Blight
potato_early_blight.summary: Caused by Alternaria solani. Dark, leathery spots with concentric rings on lower leaves.
actions: Apply mancozeb or chlorothalonil fungicide preventively.
Remove and destroy infected leaves.
Maintain adequate plant nutrition — stressed plants are more susceptible.
Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 2–3 years.
Avoid overhead irrigation.

bean_angular_spot.title: Bean Angular Leaf Spot
bean_angular_spot.summary: Caused by Phaeoisariopsis griseola. Angular, grey-brown spots on leaves constrained by leaf veins.
actions: Use certified disease-free seed.
Apply mancozeb or thiram-based fungicide at first symptom.
Rotate with non-legume crops for at least one season.
Remove and destroy crop residues after harvest.
Plant tolerant varieties (e.g., CAL 96, RWR 719).

bean_rust.title: Bean Rust
bean_rust.summary: Caused by Uromyces appendiculatus. Rust-coloured pustules on leaf undersides.
actions: Apply tebuconazole or mancozeb fungicide at pustule appearance.
Increase plant spacing to improve air circulation.
Avoid late planting seasons associated with high humidity.
Use rust-tolerant varieties where available.
Remove and destroy infected plant debris after harvest.

bean_common_mosaic.title: Bean Common Mosaic Virus (BCMV)
bean_common_mosaic.summary: Seed-borne and aphid-transmitted virus. Yellow-green mosaic and plant dwarfing.
actions: Use certified, BCMV-indexed seed from reputable sources.
Plant BCMV-resistant varieties (e.g., Lyamungu 85).
Control aphid vectors with insecticides or reflective mulch.
Remove infected plants to reduce spread.
Do not save seed from infected plants.

coffee_leaf_rust.title: Coffee Leaf Rust (CLR)
coffee_leaf_rust.summary: Caused by Hemileia vastatrix. Orange-yellow powdery pustules on leaf undersides; causes heavy defoliation.
actions: Apply copper-based or triazole fungicide preventively at onset of rainy season.
Use CLR-resistant/tolerant varieties (Ruiru 11, Batian, Catimor).
Prune to improve canopy air flow.
Maintain adequate nutrition — balanced N, K reduces susceptibility.
Dispose of fallen infected leaves.

coffee_berry_disease.title: Coffee Berry Disease (CBD)
coffee_berry_disease.summary: Caused by Colletotrichum kahawae. Black, mummified berries. Major loss in highland East Africa.
actions: Apply copper oxychloride spray at first berry set and repeat every 3 weeks.
Harvest and remove all ripe and overripe berries.
Use CBD-resistant varieties (Ruiru 11, Batian).
Prune coffee trees to reduce canopy density.
Collect and burn mummified berries.

coffee_wilt.title: Coffee Wilt Disease (CWD) / Tracheomycosis
coffee_wilt.summary: Caused by Gibberella xylarioides. Causes rapid wilting and death of branches; blue-green staining of wood.
actions: Rogue and burn infected trees immediately.
Sterilise pruning tools between trees.
Plant tolerant varieties (Timor Hybrid derivates).
Avoid wounding stems; borer entry creates infection points.
Report severe outbreaks to national coffee authority.

rice_blast.title: Rice Blast
rice_blast.summary: Caused by Magnaporthe oryzae. Diamond-shaped grey lesions with brown borders. Affects all above-ground parts.
actions: Apply tricyclazole or azoxystrobin fungicide at blast warning conditions.
Plant blast-resistant varieties (e.g., NERICA, IR64).
Avoid excessive nitrogen application — it increases blast severity.
Ensure uniform water depth in paddies.
Remove and destroy infected plant debris.

rice_brown_spot.title: Rice Brown Spot
rice_brown_spot.summary: Caused by Cochliobolus miyabeanus. Circular to oval brown spots; indicator of nutrient stress.
actions: Apply potassium and silica fertilisers to reduce susceptibility.
Seed treat with thiram or carbendazim before planting.
Apply mancozeb or iprodione at early infection stage.
Use balanced fertilisation (avoid excess nitrogen with low potassium).
Use certified disease-free seed.

rice_bacterial_blight.title: Rice Bacterial Blight (Kresek)
rice_bacterial_blight.summary: Caused by Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae. Leaf margins turn yellow then white; severe wilting in seedlings.
actions: Plant resistant varieties (e.g., IR36, WITA 4).
Apply copper bactericide at early symptom detection.
Drain fields during disease development to reduce humidity.
Use clean irrigation water; disease spreads through water.
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation.

sweet_potato_virus.title: Sweet Potato Virus Disease (SPVD)
sweet_potato_virus.summary: Co-infection of SPCSV and SPFMV, whitefly-transmitted. Severe mosaic, distortion and stunting.
actions: Use virus-free planting material from certified clean sources.
Control whitefly vectors with neem extracts or insecticides.
Rogue out severely infected plants.
Plant resistant varieties (e.g., SPK004 'Kakamega').
Avoid planting near old, infected sweet potato plots.

sweet_potato_weevil.title: Sweet Potato Weevil
sweet_potato_weevil.summary: Cylas spp. Larvae tunnel into roots causing bitterness and rendering tubers inedible.
actions: Hill up around plants to cover exposed roots.
Irrigate regularly — weevils are worse in drought conditions.
Harvest at maturity; do not leave in ground after maturity.
Use weevil-resistant varieties (e.g., Naspot 1-12 series).
Apply entomopathogenic nematodes as biological control.

sorghum_leaf_blight.title: Sorghum Leaf Blight
sorghum_leaf_blight.summary: Caused by Exserohilum turcicum. Oval-shaped tan lesions with yellow halos on leaves.
actions: Plant resistant varieties.
Apply mancozeb or propiconazole fungicide at first symptom.
Rotate sorghum with non-grass crops.
Remove and burn infected crop debris.
Ensure adequate plant spacing.

sorghum_downy_mildew.title: Sorghum Downy Mildew
sorghum_downy_mildew.summary: Caused by Peronosclerospora sorghi. Pale green-yellow leaves with white downy growth; sterile plants.
actions: Use metalaxyl seed treatment before planting.
Plant resistant varieties.
Rogue and destroy systemically infected plants early.
Rotate with non-grass crops for 2+ years.
Avoid planting in fields with history of SDM.

wheat_rust.title: Wheat Stem/Leaf Rust
wheat_rust.summary: Caused by Puccinia spp. Rust-coloured pustules on leaves and stems. Can cause total crop loss.
actions: Plant rust-resistant varieties (recommend varieties released by CIMMYT).
Apply triazole fungicide (tebuconazole) preventively.
Monitor fields regularly during cool, humid weather.
Harvest early in severe epidemics to limit yield loss.
Avoid growing susceptible varieties in regions with known rust pressure.

wheat_septoria.title: Wheat Septoria Leaf Blotch
wheat_septoria.summary: Caused by Zymoseptoria tritici. Tan lesions with yellow halos and black pycnidia on lower leaves.
actions: Apply fungicide (azoxystrobin + propiconazole) at GS31–39.
Use resistant or partially resistant varieties.
Rotate with non-wheat crops for at least one year.
Bury infected stubble through ploughing.
Avoid sowing in very late season with high rainfall.

groundnut_leaf_spot.title: Groundnut Leaf Spot (Early/Late)
groundnut_leaf_spot.summary: Caused by Cercosporidium personatum (late) or Cercospora arachidicola (early). Dark spots causing premature defoliation.
actions: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicide every 14 days from 30 days after planting.
Use resistant groundnut varieties (ICGV series from ICRISAT).
Rotate with non-legume crops.
Collect and destroy crop residues.
Maintain proper spacing and earthing up.

groundnut_rosette.title: Groundnut Rosette Virus
groundnut_rosette.summary: Transmitted by aphid Aphis craccivora. Severe stunting, chlorotic or green mosaic; no cure.
actions: Control aphid vector with early insecticide application.
Use resistant/tolerant varieties (ICGV-SM 99568, RMP-91).
Plant early — late planting increases aphid pressure.
Remove and destroy infected plants.
Use reflective silver mulch to deter aphids.

soybean_rust.title: Soybean Asian Rust
soybean_rust.summary: Caused by Phakopsora pachyrhizi. Tan to brown pustules on leaf undersides; can cause 10–80% yield loss.
actions: Apply triazole fungicide (tebuconazole, propiconazole) at first pustule observation.
Scout regularly especially in the reproductive stages R1–R5.
Plant early to reduce exposure to late-season epidemic build-up.
Use partially resistant varieties.
Avoid planting near volunteer soybeans or alternative hosts.

onion_purple_blotch.title: Onion Purple Blotch
onion_purple_blotch.summary: Caused by Alternaria porri. Small, white lesions turning purple with yellow borders.
actions: Apply mancozeb or iprodione fungicide at 10-day intervals.
Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip or furrow irrigation.
Space plants adequately to improve air circulation.
Remove and dispose of infected leaves.
Use disease-free transplants.

cabbage_black_rot.title: Cabbage Black Rot
cabbage_black_rot.summary: Caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. campestris. V-shaped yellow lesions at leaf margins; black vascular tissue.
actions: Use certified disease-free seed.
Rotate with non-brassica crops for 2–3 years.
Apply copper bactericide preventively.
Avoid irrigation wetting foliage.
Remove and destroy infected plant material.

apple_scab.title: Apple Scab
apple_scab.summary: Caused by Venturia inaequalis. Olive-green spots on leaves and fruit; distorted, scabby fruit.
actions: Apply captan or myclobutanil fungicide from green tip through petal fall on a 7–10 day cycle.
Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn.
Prune for good air circulation in canopy.
Plant scab-resistant varieties (e.g., Topaz, Florina).
Avoid wetting foliage with irrigation.

apple_black_rot.title: Apple Black Rot
apple_black_rot.summary: Caused by Botryosphaeria obtusa. Purple spots on leaves, black mummified fruit, cankers on branches.
actions: Remove and destroy mummified fruit and infected branches.
Apply captan fungicide from pink bud through second cover.
Prune out dead wood and cankers during dormancy.
Maintain tree vigour with proper nutrition.
Avoid wounding; paint pruning cuts.

apple_cedar_rust.title: Apple Cedar Apple Rust
apple_cedar_rust.summary: Caused by Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. Orange-yellow spots on leaves; requires juniper alternate host.
actions: Apply myclobutanil or mancozeb fungicide from pink bud through cover sprays.
Remove nearby juniper/cedar trees (alternate host) if feasible.
Plant rust-resistant apple varieties.
Scout in spring for jelly-like orange spore masses on junipers.
Time fungicide sprays with orange spore release periods.

grape_black_rot.title: Grape Black Rot
grape_black_rot.summary: Caused by Guignardia bidwellii. Tan, circular spots with dark borders on leaves; hard, black shrivelled fruit.
actions: Apply myclobutanil, captan, or mancozeb from bud swell through veraison.
Remove and destroy mummified berries and infected canes.
Train vines for good air circulation.
Scout weekly during cool, wet weather.
Spray applications critical 2 weeks before and after bloom.

grape_esca.title: Grape Esca / Wood Diseases
grape_esca.summary: Complex of wood-decaying fungi (Phaeomoniella, Phaeoacremonium). Tiger-stripe leaves, sudden shoot death.
actions: Prune during dry weather and seal large pruning wounds.
Remove and destroy infected wood.
Avoid large pruning cuts; stagger removal over multiple seasons.
Apply bio-fungicide (Trichoderma) to pruning wounds.
No fully effective chemical treatment; focus on prevention.

grape_leaf_blight.title: Grape Leaf Blight (Isariopsis)
grape_leaf_blight.summary: Caused by Pseudocercospora vitis. Reddish-brown angular spots on upper leaf surface.
actions: Apply copper-based fungicide or mancozeb at first symptom.
Improve canopy ventilation through shoot positioning and leaf removal.
Remove infected leaves and dispose away from vineyard.
Maintain spray coverage on upper and lower leaf surfaces.
Avoid excessive irrigation.

pepper_bacterial_spot.title: Pepper Bacterial Spot
pepper_bacterial_spot.summary: Caused by Xanthomonas spp. Water-soaked spots on leaves and fruit; yellow halos.
actions: Use certified disease-free seed.
Apply copper hydroxide bactericide preventively.
Avoid working in fields when plants are wet.
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry.
Plant resistant varieties where available.

citrus_greening.title: Citrus Greening (Huanglongbing / HLB)
citrus_greening.summary: Caused by Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus; transmitted by Asian citrus psyllid. Blotchy mottle on leaves, green, misshapen fruit.
actions: Control Asian citrus psyllid with systemic insecticides (imidacloprid, thiamethoxam).
Remove and destroy all infected trees.
Use certified disease-free nursery trees.
Never move plant material from HLB-affected areas.
There is no cure — focus on psyllid control and replanting with clean material.

mango_anthracnose.title: Mango Anthracnose
mango_anthracnose.summary: Caused by Colletotrichum gloeosporioides. Black lesions on leaves, flowers, and fruit; major post-harvest issue.
actions: Apply copper oxychloride or mancozeb at flowering and fruit set.
Post-harvest hot water treatment (52°C for 5 minutes) to reduce fruit infection.
Prune for good canopy ventilation.
Collect and destroy infected plant material.
Use post-harvest fungicide dip (thiabendazole) for stored fruit.

mango_powdery_mildew.title: Mango Powdery Mildew
mango_powdery_mildew.summary: Caused by Oidium mangiferae. White powdery coating on flowers, young leaves and fruitlets.
actions: Apply wettable sulfur or triadimefon fungicide at early flowering.
Spray every 10–14 days during flowering period.
Prune dead wood and improve canopy ventilation.
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation.
Spray when no rain forecast for 24 hours for best coverage.

avocado_root_rot.title: Avocado Root Rot (Phytophthora cinnamomi)
avocado_root_rot.summary: The most destructive avocado disease worldwide. Feeder roots turn dark brown; tree yellows and wilts progressively.
actions: Improve soil drainage — plant on raised beds or mounds.
Apply phosphonate fungicide (potassium phosphonate) as a trunk spray or soil drench.
Avoid overwatering; install drip irrigation.
Use rootstocks with partial resistance (Dusa, Bounty).
Never move soil or plant material from infected orchards.

avocado_anthracnose.title: Avocado Anthracnose
avocado_anthracnose.summary: Caused by Colletotrichum gloeosporioides. Post-harvest black sunken lesions on fruit skin.
actions: Apply copper-based fungicide at flowering and during fruit development.
Post-harvest hot water dip (45–50°C, 10 min) to reduce field latent infections.
Prune canopy to improve air circulation and light penetration.
Handle fruit carefully to avoid wounds at harvest.
Use post-harvest thiabendazole or prochloraz dip.

avocado_dieback.title: Avocado Stem Canker / Dieback
avocado_dieback.summary: Caused by Botryosphaeria and Phytophthora spp. Dark, sunken cankers on branches; dieback from tips.
actions: Prune and destroy affected branches below the canker margin.
Paint all pruning cuts with copper-based wound paint.
Improve tree vigour through balanced nutrition.
Avoid any stress (drought, waterlogging, pest damage) that predisposes trees.
Apply phosphonate or copper fungicide to affected areas.

tea_blister_blight.title: Tea Blister Blight
tea_blister_blight.summary: Caused by Exobasidium vexans. Small pale blisters on young leaves; severely affects yield of young flushes.
actions: Apply copper-based fungicide (copper oxychloride) every 7–10 days during wet flush seasons.
Pluck affected leaves and destroy them away from the field.
Prune for good canopy ventilation.
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation.
Plant tolerant clones where available.

tea_grey_blight.title: Tea Grey Blight
tea_grey_blight.summary: Caused by Pestalotiopsis theae. Grey-brown lesions with yellow borders on mature leaves; common in stressed bushes.
actions: Improve soil drainage and nutrition to reduce bush stress.
Apply copper fungicide at first sign of lesions.
Remove severely affected leaves.
Maintain proper pruning cycles.
Avoid physical injury to leaves during plucking.

tea_root_rot.title: Tea Red Root Rot / Armillaria
tea_root_rot.summary: Caused by Armillaria spp. Yellowing and wilting of bushes; white mycelial sheets under bark of roots.
actions: Uproot and burn infected bushes including all root material.
Expose root stumps and collar region to sunlight.
Apply Trichoderma bio-fungicide to soil around affected areas.
Allow fallow period of 2–3 years before replanting.
Use certified disease-free planting material.

cowpea_mosaic.title: Cowpea Mosaic Virus (CPMV)
cowpea_mosaic.summary: Transmitted by aphids and beetles. Mosaic mottling, leaf distortion, and reduced pod fill.
actions: Use certified CPMV-free seed.
Control insect vectors with insecticides (imidacloprid) at early seedling stage.
Plant resistant/tolerant cowpea varieties (TVu series).
Remove and destroy infected plants early.
Use reflective silver mulch to repel aphid vectors.

cowpea_brown_blotch.title: Cowpea Brown Blotch
cowpea_brown_blotch.summary: Caused by Colletotrichum truncatum. Brown necrotic blotches on leaves and pods; seed discolouration.
actions: Use disease-free certified seed.
Apply mancozeb or thiram fungicide at first symptom.
Rotate with non-legume crops.
Remove and destroy infected crop debris.
Harvest and dry pods quickly in wet seasons.

pigeon_pea_wilt.title: Pigeon Pea Fusarium Wilt
pigeon_pea_wilt.summary: Caused by Fusarium udum. Yellowing and wilting of shoots; brown discolouration of vascular tissue.
actions: Plant Fusarium-resistant varieties (ICPL series from ICRISAT).
Avoid replanting pigeon pea in infected soil for 3+ years.
Remove and destroy wilted plants immediately.
Treat seed with carbendazim or Trichoderma before planting.
Improve soil drainage — waterlogging worsens the disease.

pigeon_pea_sterility.title: Pigeon Pea Sterility Mosaic Disease
pigeon_pea_sterility.summary: Caused by Pigeon Pea Sterility Mosaic Virus (PPSMV), spread by eriophyid mites. Plants become sterile (no pods).
actions: Control eriophyid mite vector with sulfur or abamectin spray.
Plant resistant/tolerant varieties (ICPL 87119, Asha).
Rogue infected plants immediately.
Avoid early planting when mite populations are high.
Use clean planting material.

eggplant_leaf_spot.title: Eggplant (Brinjal) Phomopsis Blight
eggplant_leaf_spot.summary: Caused by Phomopsis vexans. Circular brown spots on leaves; stem cankers and fruit rot.
actions: Apply mancozeb or copper fungicide every 7–10 days in wet weather.
Use certified disease-free seed.
Rotate with non-solanaceous crops.
Remove and destroy infected plant material.
Avoid overhead irrigation.

eggplant_bacterial_wilt.title: Eggplant Bacterial Wilt
eggplant_bacterial_wilt.summary: Caused by Ralstonia solanacearum. Rapid wilting without leaf yellowing; white bacterial ooze in cut stems.
actions: Remove and destroy wilted plants immediately — do not compost.
Avoid waterlogging; improve soil drainage.
Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3–4 years.
Use grafted seedlings on resistant Solanum rootstocks.
Sanitise tools with 1% sodium hypochlorite between plants.

watermelon_anthracnose.title: Watermelon Anthracnose
watermelon_anthracnose.summary: Caused by Colletotrichum orbiculare. Dark, sunken lesions on leaves, stem, and fruit; fruit becomes unmarketable.
actions: Apply chlorothalonil or azoxystrobin fungicide preventively.
Use certified disease-free seed; treat with thiram before planting.
Rotate cucurbit crops on a 2-year cycle.
Remove and destroy infected vines and fruit.
Avoid overhead irrigation.

watermelon_gummy_stem_blight.title: Watermelon Gummy Stem Blight
watermelon_gummy_stem_blight.summary: Caused by Didymella bryoniae. Yellow-brown crown lesions oozing gummy exudate; rapid vine death.
actions: Apply thiophanate-methyl or azoxystrobin fungicide at first symptom.
Use certified disease-free seed.
Remove and destroy infected plant material.
Rotate with non-cucurbit crops for 2+ years.
Avoid injuries to stems at transplanting.

sugarcane_smut.title: Sugarcane Smut
sugarcane_smut.summary: Caused by Sporisorium scitamineum. Black whip-like structures replace the growing point; heavily infected fields have >50% loss.
actions: Plant smut-resistant varieties (e.g., N14, NCo 376).
Use certified disease-free setts for planting.
Hot-water treat setts (50°C for 30 minutes) before planting.
Remove and burn smut whips before they rupture and spread spores.
Rogue heavily infected stools.

sugarcane_red_rot.title: Sugarcane Red Rot
sugarcane_red_rot.summary: Caused by Colletotrichum falcatum. Internal reddening of stalk tissue with white spots; sour fermented smell.
actions: Plant resistant varieties.
Use disease-free setts; treat with carbendazim fungicide.
Harvest at maturity — over-mature cane is more susceptible.
Avoid mechanical damage during harvest and transport.
Destroy crop debris after harvest.

cocoa_black_pod.title: Cocoa Black Pod Disease
cocoa_black_pod.summary: Caused by Phytophthora megakarya (West/Central Africa) and P. palmivora. Black, rotting pods; can destroy entire harvest.
actions: Apply copper oxychloride or metalaxyl fungicide every 2–3 weeks during wet season.
Remove and destroy all infected pods including those on the ground.
Prune to reduce canopy density and improve air circulation.
Harvest ripe pods promptly to reduce infection sites.
Use partially resistant varieties (CCN51, some CRIG clones).

cocoa_swollen_shoot.title: Cocoa Swollen Shoot Virus (CSSV)
cocoa_swollen_shoot.summary: Transmitted by mealybugs. Swelling of shoots and roots; red vein-banding on young leaves; death within 2–3 years.
actions: Cut and remove all infected trees including stumps (Cutting Out policy).
Control mealybug vectors with insecticides.
Replant with CSSV-tolerant hybrid varieties.
Establish a buffer zone of 50+ meters around outbreaks.
Do not transport planting material from affected areas.

papaya_ringspot.title: Papaya Ringspot Virus (PRSV)
papaya_ringspot.summary: Aphid-transmitted. Yellow mosaic on leaves, dark green rings on fruit surface; severely reduces marketability.
actions: Plant certified virus-free seedlings from tissue culture.
Control aphid vectors with mineral oil or insecticides.
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately.
Use transgenic PRSV-resistant varieties where available and permitted.
Establish new orchards far from infected plantations.

papaya_anthracnose.title: Papaya Anthracnose
papaya_anthracnose.summary: Caused by Colletotrichum gloeosporioides. Post-harvest circular water-soaked spots that turn brown; serious storage problem.
actions: Apply copper or mancozeb fungicide during fruit development.
Post-harvest hot water dip (48°C, 20 minutes).
Handle fruit carefully to avoid wounds.
Store at 10–13°C to slow disease development.
Prune for good canopy ventilation.

citrus_canker.title: Citrus Canker
citrus_canker.summary: Caused by Xanthomonas citri subsp. citri. Raised, corky lesions with water-soaked margins on leaves, twigs and fruit.
actions: Apply copper bactericide (copper hydroxide) preventively.
Remove and destroy infected leaves and fruit.
Quarantine infected orchards; do not move plant material.
Windbreaks reduce spread through rain-splash and wind-driven rain.
Report to plant health authority — notifiable disease in many countries.

citrus_melanose.title: Citrus Melanose
citrus_melanose.summary: Caused by Diaporthe citri. Small, raised dark-brown spots on leaves and fruit; mainly a cosmetic problem.
actions: Apply copper fungicide preventively before and after rain during young fruit stage.
Prune dead wood (the primary source of spores).
Remove pruning debris from orchard.
Maintain tree vigour through good nutrition.
Melanose-affected fruit is safe to eat but reduces market value.

yam_anthracnose.title: Yam Anthracnose
yam_anthracnose.summary: Caused by Colletotrichum gloeosporioides. Irregular dark lesions on leaves; premature defoliation reducing tuber yield.
actions: Apply mancozeb or copper fungicide every 2 weeks during vine establishment.
Use disease-free planting material (seed yam).
Stake vines to improve air circulation.
Remove and destroy infected plant parts.
Avoid wounding tubers during planting.

yam_mosaic.title: Yam Mosaic Virus (YMV)
yam_mosaic.summary: Aphid-transmitted. Mosaic mottling, leaf distortion and stunted plants; tuber yield reduced by 30–60%.
actions: Use certified virus-free seed yam from reputable sources.
Control aphid vectors with insecticides at emergence.
Remove and destroy infected plants.
Avoid replanting from virus-infected fields.
Use tissue-culture derived planting material where available.

taro_leaf_blight.title: Taro Leaf Blight
taro_leaf_blight.summary: Caused by Phytophthora colocasiae. Large water-soaked lesions rapidly killing leaves; can collapse entire planting.
actions: Apply metalaxyl-mancozeb or copper fungicide preventively.
Remove and destroy infected leaves immediately.
Plant tolerant varieties (e.g., Harosi, Tausala Pink).
Improve drainage to reduce leaf wetness.
Space plants for good air circulation.

sunflower_downy_mildew.title: Sunflower Downy Mildew
sunflower_downy_mildew.summary: Caused by Plasmopara halstedii. Yellowing of systemically infected seedlings; white downy growth on leaf undersides.
actions: Use metalaxyl seed treatment before planting.
Plant resistant/tolerant hybrid varieties.
Rotate with non-sunflower crops for 2+ years.
Avoid planting in known infected fields.
Destroy infected plants at first sign.

sunflower_rust.title: Sunflower Rust
sunflower_rust.summary: Caused by Puccinia helianthi. Rust-coloured pustules on both leaf surfaces; premature leaf death.
actions: Plant resistant hybrid varieties.
Apply triazole fungicide (tebuconazole) preventively.
Scout regularly from flowering stage.
Destroy crop stubble after harvest.
Rotate with non-host crops.

cotton_boll_rot.title: Cotton Boll Rot
cotton_boll_rot.summary: Caused by multiple fungi (Fusarium, Rhizoctonia, Phytophthora). Open bolls turn brown and rot; major yield loss.
actions: Control boll weevil and other insects to reduce entry wounds.
Apply copper or mancozeb fungicide when wet conditions favour infection.
Harvest bolls promptly when they open.
Improve field drainage.
Plant short-season varieties to avoid late-season wet weather.

cotton_leaf_curl.title: Cotton Leaf Curl Virus (CLCuV)
cotton_leaf_curl.summary: Whitefly-transmitted. Upward curling of leaves, dark vein thickening, stunted plants; major problem in South Asia and East Africa.
actions: Control whitefly vector aggressively with imidacloprid or thiamethoxam.
Plant CLCuV-resistant varieties.
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor whitefly populations.
Rogue infected plants early in the season.
Avoid overlapping cotton seasons (summer-on-summer) which build up virus.
